title: Least Common Multiple

projects.title: Unit 1 Project — Number System in Action
projects.desc: Apply factors, decimals, and division to a real-world capstone challenge for Unit 1.

projects.title: Treasure Hunter — GCF & LCM
projects.desc: Hunt treasure by finding the least common multiple of two numbers (6.NS.4).

projects.title: Factor & Multiple Review Lab
projects.desc: Practice LCM, GCF, and multiples in an interactive review lab (6.NS.4).

projects.title: Prime Station: The Factor Code (Part 1)
projects.desc: Interactive graphic novel: use factors and multiples to crack the code (6.NS.4).

contentObjective: I can find the least common multiple (LCM) of two numbers by listing or comparing their multiples.
languageObjective: I can explain how I found the LCM using the words multiple, common multiple, skip counting, and least common multiple.

turnAndTalk.question: Astronaut Reyes checks oxygen every 4 hours and Chen checks water every 6 hours, both starting at 6:00 AM. Why isn't the next shared check simply 10 hours later (4 + 6)?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The next shared check is a common multiple of 4 and 6, not their sum.

stems.en: Their checks line up at a ___ of both 4 and 6.
stems.es: Sus revisiones coinciden en un ___ de 4 y de 6.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students reason that each astronaut repeats on multiples of their own number, so they only overlap at a common multiple, and adding 4 + 6 does not land on both schedules.
turnAndTalk.extend: If Reyes checked every 4 hours and Chen every 8 hours, when would they next line up? Explain why the answer is not 4 + 8.
extendStems: They line up after ___ hours because ___.
It is not 12 because the LCM of 4 and 8 is ___, since ___.

turnAndTalk.question: You skip counted the multiples of 4 and 6. How did you find the least common multiple from your lists?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The LCM is the first multiple that appears in both lists.

stems.en: The multiples of 4 are ___ and the multiples of 6 are ___.
stems.es: Los múltiplos de 4 son ___ y los múltiplos de 6 son ___.

stems.en: The least common multiple is ___ because it is the ___ shared multiple.
stems.es: El mínimo común múltiplo es ___ porque es el múltiplo compartido más ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students skip count 4, 8, 12 and 6, 12 and identify 12 as the LCM because it is the smallest number appearing in both lists.
turnAndTalk.extend: Will the LCM of two numbers ever be smaller than the larger number? Justify using 4 and 6.
extendStems: The LCM is never smaller than the larger number because ___.
For 4 and 6 the LCM is ___, which is ___ than 6.

turnAndTalk.question: Hot dogs come in packs of 8 and buns in packs of 6. How does the LCM help you buy the fewest of each so none are left over?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The LCM of 8 and 6 is the smallest matching total of dogs and buns.

stems.en: I need to buy ___ hot dogs and ___ buns because the LCM of 8 and 6 is ___.
stems.es: Necesito comprar ___ hot dogs y ___ panes porque el mcm de 8 y 6 es ___.

stems.en: That means ___ packs of hot dogs and ___ packs of buns.
stems.es: Eso significa ___ paquetes de hot dogs y ___ paquetes de panes.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students compute LCM(8, 6) = 24, so 3 packs of hot dogs and 4 packs of buns give 24 each with nothing left over.
turnAndTalk.extend: Why is the LCM the FEWEST you can buy and not just any common multiple like 48? Explain the trade-off.
extendStems: The LCM gives the fewest because ___.
Buying 48 of each would also work, but ___.

turnAndTalk.question: How do you decide whether a real-world problem needs the LCM or the GCF?
turnAndTalk.kernel: LCM answers 'when do repeating events meet'; GCF answers 'how to split into equal groups'.

stems.en: I use the LCM when ___, but the GCF when ___.
stems.es: Uso el mcm cuando ___, pero el MCD cuando ___.

stems.en: The astronaut schedule needs the ___ because ___.
stems.es: El horario de los astronautas necesita el ___ porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain LCM is for repeating/lining-up events (schedules, packs) while GCF is for splitting into equal groups, and correctly tag the schedule as an LCM problem.
turnAndTalk.extend: Write your own short word problem that needs the LCM, then explain why GCF would give the wrong answer.
extendStems: My problem is ___, and it needs the LCM because ___.
Using the GCF would be wrong because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Least Common Multiple, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The LCM is the FIRST number that shows up in both numbers' multiple lists.

stems.en: The multiples of 4 are ___ and the multiples of 6 are ___.
stems.es: Los múltiplos de 4 son ___ y los múltiplos de 6 son ___.

stems.en: I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.NS.4 — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

vocabulary.definition: What you get when you multiply a number by 1, 2, 3, and so on.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Lo que obtienes al multiplicar un número por 1, 2, 3, y así.
vocabulary.cloze: The product of a number and any whole number is a ___ of that number.

examples.why: There is no whole number times 4 that equals 9.

vocabulary.definition: The smallest number that two or more numbers both go into.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número más pequeño al que llegan dos o más números al multiplicar.
vocabulary.visual: Multiples of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24. Multiples of 6: 6, 12, 18, 24. First match: 12 → LCM = 12

sentences.text: The least common multiple of 4 and 6 is 12.
The least common multiple of 4 and 6 is 24.
The least common multiple of 4 and 6 is 2.

vocabulary.definition: A number that two or more numbers both go into.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un número al que llegan dos o más números al multiplicar.
vocabulary.visual: 12, 24, and 36 are all common multiples of both 3 and 4
vocabulary.cloze: A multiple that two or more numbers share is a ___ multiple.

vocabulary.definition: Counting by a number, like 2, 4, 6, to list its multiples.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Contar de un número en un número, como 2, 4, 6, para listar sus múltiplos.
vocabulary.visual: Skip count by 6: 6, 12, 18, 24, 30 — each jump adds 6
vocabulary.cloze: Counting forward by a number other than 1, like 6, 12, 18, is called ___.

vocabulary.definition: Writing a number as prime numbers multiplied together. It helps you find the LCM.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Escribir un número como números primos multiplicados. Ayuda a encontrar el mcm.
vocabulary.visual: 4 = 2 × 2, 6 = 2 × 3. LCM uses the highest power of each prime: 2² × 3 = 12

launch.narrative: Astronaut Reyes checks the oxygen system every 4 hours. Astronaut Chen checks the water recycler every 6 hours. They both start their shifts at 6:00 AM. When is the next time both astronauts will be checking their systems at the same time?
launch.contextImage: Space station control room with two astronauts at separate monitoring stations with digital clocks
noticePrompts: How often does each astronaut check their system?
What times will each astronaut be doing checks?
wonderPrompts: When will both schedules line up at the same time?
Will their schedules ever line up more than once?

visual.title: Multiples of 4 and 6
visual.caption: 12 is the first multiple of BOTH 4 and 6 → LCM = 12

conceptIntro.heading: What is the least common multiple (LCM)?
conceptIntro.intro: A multiple is what you get when you count by a number: 4, 8, 12, 16. The least common multiple is the smallest multiple that two numbers share.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: The LCM is the FIRST number that shows up in both numbers' multiple lists.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: I want the LCM of 4 and 6. I skip count the multiples of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24.
Then I skip count the multiples of 6: 6, 12, 18, 24.
I look for the first number in BOTH lists. 12 is in both lists, and it comes before 24.
So the LCM of 4 and 6 is 12. I check: 12 ÷ 4 = 3 and 12 ÷ 6 = 2, so both go into 12 evenly.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Let's find the LCM of 3 and 5. What are the multiples of 3? (3, 6, 9, 12, 15.)
What are the multiples of 5? (5, 10, 15.) What is the first number in both lists? (15.)
So the LCM of 3 and 5 is 15.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Next, you will sort multiples to find the common multiples of two numbers and pick the smallest one.
Remember: the LCM is the SMALLEST multiple the two numbers share.

explore.instructions: Here are the first several multiples of 4 and 6 mixed together. Sort them — which are common multiples of BOTH 4 and 6, and which are multiples of only one?

discourse.prompt: What is the least common multiple of 4 and 6? How did sorting the multiples help you find it?
discourse.sentenceFrame: The LCM of 4 and 6 is ___ because it is the smallest number in the ___ group.

optional.stem: Stop 1 — Oxygen Bay: The oxygen scrubber cycles every 4 minutes and the air filter cycles every 6 minutes. They both just ran together. After how many minutes do they next run together?
optional.explanation: Skip count: multiples of 4 are 4, 8, 12; multiples of 6 are 6, 12. The first shared multiple is LCM(4, 6) = 12 minutes.

optional.stem: Stop 2 — Docking Ring: A supply pod arrives every 8 minutes and a cargo shuttle arrives every 12 minutes. They just docked together. After how many minutes do they next dock together?
optional.explanation: LCM(8, 12) = 24. Multiples of 8: 8, 16, 24; multiples of 12: 12, 24. The first shared multiple is 24 minutes.

optional.label: Stop 3 — Engineering Deck: Match each pair of station systems to the LCM of their cycle times (when they next sync).

optional.instructions: Stop 4 — Mission Control: Each clue points to your next stop. Sort each crew schedule by whether the two systems sync again in UNDER 25 minutes or 25 MINUTES OR MORE. Skip count to find each LCM.

categories.label: Sync again in 25 minutes or more

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — Crew Member's Schedule Sync Mistake

workedExample.label: Problem
workedExample.work: The water recycler cycles every 8 minutes and the heat exchanger cycles every 12 minutes. They just ran together. When do they next sync?

workedExample.label: Pick the number to use
workedExample.work: The greatest factor that 8 and 12 share is 4.

optional.correctWork: Step 4 should find the LEAST COMMON MULTIPLE, not the greatest common FACTOR. 'When do repeating events line up?' calls for the LCM. The first multiple in both lists is 24, so they next sync in 24 minutes.
hints: A scavenger-hunt schedule asks WHEN two repeating events meet again — that is an LCM problem, not a GCF problem.
GCF (4) splits things into equal groups; LCM lines up repeating cycles. Look at both multiple lists and find the smallest number that appears in both: 24.

approaching.stem: What is the LCM of 3 and 5?
approaching.explanation: Multiples of 3: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, ... Multiples of 5: 5, 10, 15, ... The smallest common multiple is 15.

approaching.stem: What is the LCM of 6 and 8?
approaching.explanation: Multiples of 6: 6, 12, 18, 24, ... Multiples of 8: 8, 16, 24, ... The smallest common multiple is 24.

approaching.stem: What is the LCM of 4 and 10?
approaching.explanation: Multiples of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, ... Multiples of 10: 10, 20, ... The smallest common multiple is 20.

approaching.instructions: On the number line from 0 to 30, mark the multiples of 5 and multiples of 6. Find where they first overlap — that is the LCM.
approaching.label: Find the LCM of 5 and 6 on the number line

approaching.stem: What is the LCM of 2 and 7?
approaching.explanation: Since 2 and 7 share no common factors (they are relatively prime), LCM = 2 × 7 = 14.

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Least Common Multiple.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: The LCM is the FIRST number that shows up in both numbers' multiple lists.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: The LCM is the FIRST number that shows up in both numbers' multiple lists.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: The LCM is the FIRST number that shows up in both numbers' multiple lists.

onLevel.label: Match each pair of numbers to their LCM.
List multiples of each number until you find the LCM.

onLevel.stem: Two buses leave the station at 8:00 AM. Bus A returns every 15 minutes and Bus B returns every 20 minutes. When is the next time both buses are at the station together?
onLevel.explanation: LCM(15, 20) = 60 minutes. Both buses return together 60 minutes after 8:00 AM, at 9:00 AM.

onLevel.instructions: Sort each pair: does the LCM equal the product of the two numbers, or is the LCM less than the product?

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Least Common Multiple.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: The LCM is the FIRST number that shows up in both numbers' multiple lists.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: The LCM is the FIRST number that shows up in both numbers' multiple lists.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: The LCM is the FIRST number that shows up in both numbers' multiple lists.

extending.title: Find Jaylen's Mistake
extending.correctWork: LCM = 24 (the least common multiple is the SMALLEST shared multiple, not just any common multiple)
hints: Jaylen correctly listed the common multiples. Look at Step 5 — did he pick the smallest one?
The LCM is the LEAST (smallest) common multiple. 24 and 48 are both common multiples, but which is smaller?

extending.prompt: Hot dogs come in packs of 8 and buns come in packs of 6. What is the fewest of each you can buy so you have the same number of hot dogs and buns with none left over? Explain how the LCM solves this problem.
extending.sentenceFrame: The LCM of 8 and 6 is ___. I need to buy ___ packs of hot dogs and ___ packs of buns to get ___ of each. This works because ___.

extending.label: Use prime factorization to find the LCM. Take the highest power of each prime factor.

optionalActivity.name: LCM Schedule Sync Scavenger Hunt
optionalActivity.intro: Move around the station finding when two crew systems sync up again by skip counting to the least common multiple.

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Least Common Multiple is skipping the key idea: "The LCM is the FIRST number that shows up in both numbers' multiple lists." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: Hot dogs come in packs of 8 and buns come in packs of 6. You want to buy the exact same number of hot dogs and buns with none left over.
connect.promptQuestion: How does the LCM help you figure out the fewest hot dogs and buns to buy so they come out even?
connect.prompt: I need to buy ___ hot dogs and ___ buns because the LCM of 8 and 6 is ___.

exitTicket.stem: What is the LCM of 9 and 12?
exitTicket.explanation: Multiples of 9: 9, 18, 27, 36, ... Multiples of 12: 12, 24, 36, ... The smallest common multiple is 36.

printables.name: Multiple Race Track
printables.desc: Partners race counters down a track marking multiples; the first shared spot is the LCM.

printables.name: Least Common Multiple — Color by Number
printables.desc: Solve each problem, then color by the answer to reveal a picture.
